title: SparseMM: Head Sparsity Emerges from Visual Concept Responses in MLLMs
abstract: This paper presents a systematic investigation into how visual concepts are processed within large language models (LLMs). The authors discovered that only a small subset of attention heads (called 'visual heads') disproportionately drive visual content understanding, while the majority remain text-specialized. They found two key properties of these visual heads: (1) Sparsity - Less than 5% of attention heads are intrinsically visual-active across layers, even in models trained with extensive multimodal data; (2) Universality - Visual heads emerge consistently across different LLM architectures and generalize to multiple attention paradigms.

methodology.framework: Training-free framework to identify visual heads
methodology.anchor_task: OCR (Optical Character Recognition) to establish precise correspondence between text outputs and visual inputs
methodology.analysis_approach: Trace activation back to spatially aligned image patches to measure visual-text alignment
methodology.optimization: SparseMM - KV-Cache optimization framework that exploits visual head sparsity for accelerated inference

key_findings: Only a small subset of attention heads (visual heads) drive visual content understanding
Less than 5% of attention heads are intrinsically visual-active across layers
Visual heads emerge consistently across different LLM architectures
The identified visual heads are task-agnostic and generalize to diverse vision-language tasks
applications: Accelerated inference in multimodal tasks, especially for complex inputs like multi-turn dialogues, high-resolution images, and video/3D sequences
